title: Dog Dribbling Urine While Walking: Causes and Triage Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog leaking urine while walking? Learn about urinary incontinence, urgency levels, and why a photo can help your vet find a solution.
content_html:
TL;DR: Urinary incontinence is usually a non-emergency requiring a vet visit within 24-48 hours unless your dog cannot urinate at all. Documenting the urine's appearance and behavior with photos or video provides essential diagnostic clues for your veterinarian.

What is urinary incontinence and why is my dog leaking urine?

  • Urinary incontinence is the involuntary leaking of urine, often occurring while the dog is walking or resting without them realizing it.
  • This condition is distinct from behavioral accidents or submissive urination.
  • Common causes include a weakened bladder sphincter (hormone-responsive incontinence), urinary tract infections (UTIs), bladder stones, or neurological conditions affecting bladder control.

Is my dog leaking urine a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for a dog dribbling urine is typically Medium, requiring a vet visit within 24 to 48 hours.
  • It is generally not a life-threatening emergency unless your dog is also unable to urinate at all, is straining, or is showing signs of extreme lethargy.

How can a photo or video help my veterinarian diagnose urinary incontinence?

  • A photo allows the vet to assess the color and clarity of the urine; pink-tinged urine suggests blood, while cloudy urine may indicate a severe infection.
  • A video helps the vet determine if the dog is truly incontinent or if they are experiencing mobility issues that make it difficult to maintain a normal bathroom posture.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Urinary incontinence, characterized by the constant or intermittent unconscious passage of urine, can manifest as urine dribbling while walking. This may be due to urethral incompetence, anatomic defects such as congenital ectopic ureters, or overflow incontinence secondary to partial urethral obstruction. Non-neurogenic causes include hormone-responsive urethral incompetence, especially in neutered female dogs, and idiopathic urethral sphincter incompetence. Neurogenic causes can also lead to dribbling associated with bladder overdistension. Urge incontinence, associated with detrusor irritability, is often seen with cystitis.

Chapter: Urology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1524)

faqs.question: Could my dog's age be a factor?
faqs.answer: Yes, older dogs often develop weakened bladder muscles or cognitive dysfunction, both of which can lead to involuntary leaking.

faqs.question: Is urinary dribbling painful for dogs?
faqs.answer: The act of leaking is not usually painful, but the underlying cause, such as a bladder infection or stones, can cause significant discomfort and cramping.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to diagnose the issue?
faqs.answer: Your vet will likely perform a physical exam and request a urinalysis to check for bacteria, blood, and crystal formation.
